text: Trocar placement is demonstrated on the patient, with the surgical robot positioned on the left side.

The surgeon initiates dissection at the posterior aspect of the inferior mesenteric vein, performing a medial to lateral dissection. Hemolog clips are applied to the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then divided.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac, maintaining a position anterior to the pancreatic body using robotic dissection techniques. The left ureter is identified and preserved. Clips are placed on the main sigmoid branch of the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon clips and divides the left colic artery, then carries the dissection laterally to take down lateral attachments. The complete mobilization of the splenic flexure is accomplished, and the flexure is taken down. Following the removal of all attachments, the focus shifts to the pelvic region.

Severe adhesions between the sigmoid colon and uterus are observed due to chronic diverticular disease and previous tubal ligation surgery. The surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply dissect and separate the small colonic fragment from the uterus.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal dissection of the mesocolon towards the transaction site, using indocyanine green to assess blood supply. The dissection continues into the pelvis to prepare the rectum for transaction. The right lower quadrant robotic trocar is upsized to twelve millimeters for robotic stapler introduction. The upper rectum is stapled with one fire, and the right lower trocar is removed. The incision is upsized to 2.5 centimeters to place a wound protector, through which the distal end of the colon is exteriorized and prepared for anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a circular 28mm stapler to perform the anastomosis of the colon. An air leak test is conducted to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis, and the result is negative, indicating no leaks.

Past operative recovery was uneventful and patient discharged home on post-op day two. As a conclusion, robotic sigmoidectomy by using DaVinci XI platform is safe and feasible in patients with complicated diverticular disease.
